item.title: Noem v. Al Otro Lado

item.title: Oversight Committee Launches Investigation Into Rampant Taxpayer Fraud in California Hospice Programs
item.description: House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, led by Chairman James Comer (R-Ky.), launches investigation into California hospice programs following reports of widespread fraud. LA County represents $3.5B in hospice fraud.
item.content:encoded: WASHINGTON — Following alarming reports that California officials failed to properly safeguard federal funds, House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform Committee Chairman James Comer (R-Ky.) and Oversight Committee Republicans today launched an investigation into rampant taxpayer fraud in California's hospice programs. In a letter to California Governor Gavin Newsom, the lawmakers emphasized that the Newsom administration has been aware of state audit reports of hospice fraud for at lea

item.title: Military Drones
item.description: Drone warfare is now an industrial and budget problem. Cheap one-way systems like the Shahed-136 and the U.S. reverse-engineered FLM 136 (LUCAS)—the 'Corolla of drones'—are forcing militaries to rethink cost, survivability, and air defense economics.
item.content:encoded: Military Drones The key military question is no longer whether drones matter. It is whether a state can produce enough of them, field them fast enough, and force the enemy to spend more on defense than the attacker spends on offense. Cheap one-way attack drones changed the arithmetic. A reusable UAV has to survive. A loitering munition only has to find a target once. That difference matters because modern air defense missiles, radars, and trained crews are expensive, while a mass-produced drone

item.title: Tariffs as Regulatory Leverage
item.description: Solicitor General Sauer defended IEEPA tariffs as non-revenue regulatory measures - tools of foreign policy leverage rather than exercises of Congress's taxing power.
item.content:encoded: The $2000 Tariff Rebate Solicitor General Sauer advanced a sharp reframing of what tariffs represent under IEEPA. He argued that when imposed under emergency authority, tariffs are regulatory, not revenue-raising, and therefore fall within the President's delegated powers to "regulate importation." --- 1. Tariffs as Behavioral Tools Sauer insisted that IEEPA tariffs are most effective precisely when no one pays them. Their purpose is not to collect money, but to change foreign behavior by creati

item.title: Trump v. Vos: Update
item.description: Latest developments in Trump v. Vos case.
item.content:encoded: Update to Tariff SCOTUS Case 1. Wartime Power Delegation and Historical Use General Sauer traced the "regulate importation" phrase back to the Trading with the Enemy Act (1917), arguing it historically covered tariff-like powers used to restrict enemy trade. Justice Jackson noted this wartime context meant controlling or freezing enemy property, not raising revenue - underscoring that IEEPA's origins were defensive, not fiscal. > Sauer: wartime presidents imposed export controls (e.g., on cotton

(No. 25-250) Argued: November 5, 2025 --- At Issue Whether the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977 (IEEPA) authorizes the President to impose tariffs based on language allowing him to "regulate importation." item.title: Kei Trucks
item.description: Oregon legislation on kei truck imports and regulations.
item.content:encoded: Nimble Kei Trucks Oregon lawmakers introduced Senate Bill 1213, a bipartisan effort to legalize the on-road use of Kei trucks, positioning the state as the 31st to allow these compact Japanese utility vehicles on public roads. If passed, the bill would provide flexibility for individuals and small businesses seeking cost-effective alternatives to full-size trucks. --- Popular Kei Truck Models - Suzuki Carry - A versatile mini truck known for durability and off-road capability. - Daihatsu Hijet

The report claims these aides effectively exercised presidential authority, raising questions about the validity of decisions made during Biden's final months in office. item.title: Urban Crime
item.description: Analysis of urban crime trends and policy implications.
item.content:encoded: Hidden Urban Crime Analysis Key Findings 58% of violent crimes in urban areas never get reported to police. Young Black victims in big cities face the highest risks during evening hours. Crime Rate Statistics The violent crime rate in urban areas reached 23.3 per 1,000 persons in 2024, up from 2020 levels but stable since 2022. Hidden Patterns The data reveals hidden patterns in urban crime that official police statistics miss entirely. This underreporting phenomenon has significant implications

item.title: Sustainable Abundance
item.description: Policy discussion on sustainable economic development.
item.content:encoded: Sustainable Abundance On one side, the political left frames the rise of AI and robotics as a job-destroying threat. On the other, the right, CEOs, and markets hail it as a source of efficiency, wealth, and abundance. But the real question isn't simply how many jobs are lost or gained - it's what happens after automation makes everything cheaper, when most people no longer own the means of production. Who benefits? And what happens when the gains don't trickle down? The Two Narratives Left-wing:

But why was it never: 'provaccine activists were emboldened to impose them'?" The language used to describe policy advocacy reveals underlying assumptions about which positions are considered normative versus activis

item.title: Fiber Supplement
item.description: Health and policy implications of fiber supplementation.
item.content:encoded: The Fiber Supplement Scam: How Capitalism Commodifies Basic Nutrition Published: September 2, 2025 Capitalism is like yeast that thrives on bacteria. The bacteria are fat, sick consumers, and the yeast is corporate profits. We've taken something as simple as eating vegetables and turned it into a $4 billion market of powders, gummies, and 'functional beverages.' The irony? Most Americans get less than half the recommended daily fiber because they're eating processed foods instead of whole foods.

item.title: Liberal Housing Policy
item.description: The Center for American Progress's 'Rent Relief for Reform' plan exemplifies why liberal housing solutions fail: they bribe existing homeowners rather than trusting market signals and property rights.

The Center for American Progress thinks you deserve to live where you want. They propose bribery to get it. Build homes or else. If cities don't build enough homes, the government will bribe existing homeowners to build homes, then subsidize rents with the bribery money. > Ezra Klein argues that America's housing crisis stems from a shortage of 2-5 million homes, driven

item.title: California's $18B Problem
item.description: California faces an $18 billion budget deficit in 2026-27, larger than anticipated despite revenue improvements. The state's fiscal resilience tools are nearly exhausted, and AI-fueled stock market gains mask structural spending problems.
item.content:encoded: California's $18B Budget Deficit >The state has $14 billion in reserves and could likely find additional capacity for budgetary borrowing, if needed. That said, at this point, the state has used over $20 billion in borrowing, one‑time and temporary spending solutions are exhausted, and budget reserves are at about half of their peak. As it stands—with larger forecasted deficits and many fewer tools available to address them—California’s budget is undeniably less prepared for downturns. 2026-27 D

item.title: SF MAHA
item.description: San Francisco City Attorney files lawsuit against eleven major food manufacturers alleging they designed ultra-processed foods to be addictive and deliberately targeted children, fueling epidemics of obesity, diabetes, and fatty liver disease.

The San Francisco City Attorney filed a sweeping lawsuit against Kraft Heinz, Mondelez, PepsiCo, Coca-Cola, General Mills, Nestlé, ConAgra, Mars, Post Holdings, Kellanov, and WK Kellogg Co. The complaint alleges that these companies deliberately designed ultra-processed foods (UPF) to be addictive, targeted children with marketing, and concealed health consequences. item.title: Reparations
item.description: SF File 240701 creates a Reparations Fund as a category-four fund to receive appropriated or donated dollars; it sets the budget/legal mechanism but does not appropriate a specific amount.
item.content:encoded: Updated: December 27, 2025 San Francisco establishes a Reparations Fund Ordinance No. 258-25 (File 240701) establishes the Reparations Fund as a “category four fund” (as defined in Administrative Code § 10.100-1) to receive money appropriated or donated “to support and implement” the recommendations in the San Francisco Reparations Plan 2023, which was submitted by the African American Reparations Advisory Committee (created by Ordinance No. 259-20) and prepared by the Human Rights Commission. item.title: War Bill
item.description: NDAA FY2026 Navy shipbuilding authorization from $20.84B requested to $30.96B, including $6.34B for DDG–51 and $5.40B in labeled workforce/shipyard enhancements.
item.content:encoded: War Bill The National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2026 is a massive bill - about 3,100 pages long. The Senate took the executive branch's $20.84 billion shipbuilding request and authorized $30.96 billion instead, a $10.1 billion increase to build more destroyers. The increase centers on the DDG–51 guided-missile destroyer program—the Navy's workhorse for force projection—and introduces an explicit new line item: $5.4 billion in "wage and quality of life enhancements" for shipyard w

item.title: Noncitizen SNAP Participants
item.description: In fiscal year 2023, 68,000 noncitizens collected SNAP benefits in Massachusetts - 6.27% of the state's total SNAP participation. Analysis of citizenship composition, age demographics, state variation, and administrative challenges including Massachusetts's 14% payment error rate.
item.content:encoded: Updated: December 31, 2025 New Soda Restrictions Effective January 1, 2026 SNAP spending totaled $99.8 billion, with monthly benefits averaging $187.20 per participant. Starting January 1, 2026, new SNAP retailer compliance policies will implement stricter restrictions on soda purchases.[^10] The updated USDA Food and Nutrition Service guidelines prohibit the purchase of: - Carbonated soft drinks with added sugars - Energy drinks with added sweeteners - Sweetened bottled teas and fruit drinks Re

item.title: Congress Completes All 12 FY2026 Appropriations Bills
item.description: Congress officially completed all 12 fiscal year 2026 appropriations measures with $1.6 trillion in discretionary spending. Includes DOGE-backed reforms with 10% federal workforce reduction, $11.7B IRS rescissions, and strategic investments in defense and homeland security.
item.content:encoded: FY2026 Appropriations: $1.639T Budget with Major Workforce Cuts By January 30, the Senate passed five of the 12 fiscal year 2026 appropriations bills on a bipartisan 71-29 vote, totaling $1.639 trillion in net base discretionary spending. The package also includes a two-week continuing resolution for the Department of Homeland Security to allow time for further negotiations. That's $38.6 billion more than FY2025, driven partially offset by a 10% federal workforce cut and $11.7 billion clawed bac

item.title: Madison on the Purse: Separation of Powers and Legislative Encroachment
item.description: James Madison observed that control of the purse gives Congress a structural advantage in separation of powers that no constitutional restriction can fully prevent. An analysis of legislative encroachment, executive dependence on appropriations, and the asymmetry in constitutional design.
item.content:encoded: Madison on the Purse: Separation of Powers and Legislative Encroachment James Madison observed that an energetic executive confronts a legislature wielding control of the purse—a structural imbalance that threatens the separation of powers. The legislative power to appropriate funds gives Congress a facility for encroachment that no constitutional restriction can fully prevent. The Constitutional Framework The Appropriations Clause (Article I, Section 9) vests the power of the purse exclusively

item.title: **Interleukin-10**
item.description: IL-10 is a small protein your immune system produces to calm inflammation. When you get injured, your body floods the area with pro-inflammatory signals to fight infection and start healing. IL-10 is
item.content:encoded: Interleukin-10 IL-10 is a small protein your immune system produces to calm inflammation. When you get injured, your body floods the area with pro-inflammatory signals to fight infection and start healing. IL-10 is the off switch. It tells immune cells to stand down once the job is done. A February 2026 study in Science Immunology from Michigan State found that IL-10 is also the reason men resolve pain faster than women. Monocytes (a type of white blood cell) produce IL-10 and signal directly to

item.description: New York just hit a constitutional wall in its voucher enforcement strategy after the Appellate Division held that forcing landlords into Section 8 conflicts with Fourth Amendment protections.
item.content:encoded: Section 8 Meet Amendment 4 New York just hit a constitutional wall in its voucher enforcement strategy after the Appellate Division held that forcing landlords into Section 8 conflicts with Fourth Amendment protections. On March 5, 2026, the Appellate Division, Third Department, in Matter of People of the State of N.Y. v Commons West, LLC affirmed lower court rulings that struck down Executive Law section 296(5)(a)(1) to the extent it makes refusal to rent to Section 8 tenants unlawful. item.description: SCOTUS Rules Against IEEPA Tariff Authority
item.content:encoded: SCOTUS Rules Against IEEPA Tariff Authority In a 6-3 decision, the Supreme Court invalidated the President's tariff program under IEEPA. The Court held that "regulate importation" cannot support revenue-raising tariffs without explicit congressional authorization, reasserting Congress's exclusive control over taxation under Article I. item.title: **January Deficit Down to $94.6B**

item.content:encoded: January Deficit Down to $94.6B January's monthly budget deficit came in at $94.6 billion, the smallest monthly deficit since last spring as tax revenue surged to $560 billion and interest payments fell to $76 billion. January 2026: -$94.6B (tax revenue $560B, interest on debt $76B) The federal government collected $560 billion in tax revenue in January, up sharply from December's $484B (+15.6% MoM), while interest payments on the national debt dropped to $76 billion from $92 billion (-17.4% MoM)

item.title: **Debt %, CPI, Housing Market, and Wagner**
item.description: Dept Interest
item.content:encoded: Debt %, CPI, Housing Market, and Wagner Dept Interest The CBO's latest budget outlook projects a $1.9 trillion deficit this year. But interest costs tell the real story. Interest outlays hit $1.0 trillion in 2026 and will double to $2.1 trillion by 2036. For every dollar borrowed, 66 cents services debt. Interest will exceed defense spending every year and triple it by decade's end. This mirrors California and New York City, where long-term structural spending obligations outpaced revenue growth

item.title: **Life Expectancy and Factory Activity Up**
item.description: U.S. life expectancy reached 79 years in 2024, the highest ever recorded, driven by the largest decline in drug overdose death rates on record. Overall mortality decreased 4% year-over-year, with over
item.content:encoded: Life Expectancy and Factory Activity Up U.S. life expectancy reached 79 years in 2024, the highest ever recorded, driven by the largest decline in drug overdose death rates on record. Overall mortality decreased 4% year-over-year, with overdose deaths falling 26.2% as synthetic opioid deaths dropped 35.6%. item.description: The equal time rule requires broadcast stations to offer equal opportunities to legally qualified political candidates. Enacted in 1934 as Section 315 of the Communications Act, this provision became
item.content:encoded: "Equal Time" Rule: An Artifact of the 1934 Communications Act The equal time rule requires broadcast stations to offer equal opportunities to legally qualified political candidates. Enacted in 1934 as Section 315 of the Communications Act, this provision became a cornerstone of broadcast regulation that shaped electoral communication for nearly a century. item.content:encoded: Canada Affirms Despair A Canadian with mental illness is now claiming discrimination for being denied Medical Aid in Dying (MAID). What was framed as compassionate end-of-life care has become a cultural statement about the value of life itself. The Shift from Palliative to Preventive MAID began as a narrow policy for patients with terminal illnesses and unbearable physical suffering. item.content:encoded: Trade Deficit Collapses to $29.4B The October trade deficit plummeted to $29.4 billion from $48.1 billion in September (-39.0% MoM), marking the tightest trade balance in recent months. item.content:encoded: Warm Collectivism PGE's new Base Services Charge decouples utility usage from service, matching energy bills to customer income levels and shifting cost burdens from low-income households. item.content:encoded: Childhood Immunization Schedule Revised HHS released a comprehensive reassessment of the U.S. childhood immunization schedule, reducing the core routine vaccines from 17-18 diseases down to 11. item.content:encoded: Kicking out the people who make the Bed DHS publicly accused Hilton of a "coordinated campaign" to refuse service to ICE officers in Minneapolis. item.title: **ICE's Mobile Facial Recognition**
item.description: ICE has deployed a mobile facial recognition app called Mobile Fortify on government-issued phones used by immigration enforcement agents. The app allows agents to perform contactless biometric scans
item.content:encoded: ICE's Mobile Facial Recognition ICE has deployed a mobile facial recognition app called Mobile Fortify on government-issued phones used by immigration enforcement agents. The app allows agents to perform contactless biometric scans that match individuals against federal databases such as IDENT and the FBI's National Crime Information Center. This enables real-time identification of people during immigration enforcement operations, dramatically accelerating arrest capability. The app's existence

item.description: In 1965, a Scottish man named Angus Barbieri checked into a hospital and stopped eating solid food for 382 consecutive days. Starting at 456 pounds, he survived on only vitamins, electrolytes, tea, co
item.content:encoded: The Longest Fast: Angus Barbieri's 382-Day Record In 1965, a Scottish man named Angus Barbieri checked into a hospital and stopped eating solid food for 382 consecutive days. item.content:encoded: Massachusetts Fire Deaths Drop 12% in 2025 Massachusetts recorded 44 fire deaths in 2025, a decline from 50 in 2024. item.title: Mayor Lurie Approves Reparations
item.description: Mayor Daniel Lurie signed Ordinance No. 258-25 on December 23, 2025, establishing the Reparations Fund. The ordinance had passed the Board of Supervisors on December 16 with unanimous support.
item.content:encoded: Mayor Lurie Approves Reparations Mayor Daniel Lurie signed Ordinance No. 258-25 on December 23, 2025, establishing the Reparations Fund. The ordinance had passed the Board of Supervisors on December 16 with unanimous support. The fund is authorized to receive monies—whether appropriated or donated—to support and implement the recommendations in the San Francisco Reparations Plan 2023, submitted by the African American Reparations Advisory Committee and prepared by the Human Rights Commission. item.title: **California Faces $2.5 Billion CalFresh Bill**
item.description: Federal legislation enacted in July 2025 just handed California a major fiscal problem. Starting in federal fiscal year 2028, states will shoulder part of SNAP benefits costs—determined by their payme
item.content:encoded: California Faces $2.5 Billion CalFresh Bill Federal legislation enacted in July 2025 just handed California a major fiscal problem. Starting in federal fiscal year 2028, states will shoulder part of SNAP benefits costs—determined by their payment error rate (PER). California's 11 percent error rate means the state will pay 15 percent of its CalFresh costs if it doesn't improve. At current spending levels, that's nearly $1.8 billion annually in new benefit costs, plus another $600 million in admi

item.content:encoded: Conclusive and Preclusive Solicitor General Sauer argued that the President possesses exclusive constitutional authority over executive officials and cannot be constrained by Congress through removal restrictions. item.title: **California's Fiscal Cliff**
item.description: California's structural budget deficit has reached an unsustainable inflection point. The state faces an $18 billion gap in 2026-27, but the real problem emerges after: a $35 billion annual structural
item.content:encoded: California's Fiscal Cliff California's structural budget deficit has reached an unsustainable inflection point. The state faces an $18 billion gap in 2026-27, but the real problem emerges after: a $35 billion annual structural deficit by 2027-28. Revenue is growing, but it's fully consumed by mandatory spending from Prop 98 (schools/colleges) and Prop 2 (reserves/debt), both of which auto-scale with revenues. Rainy day funds are depleted, debt deferrals exhausted, and one-time measures exhausted

item.title: **Vermiglio**
item.content:encoded: Vermiglio Set in a remote mountain village in the Italian Alps during the waning days of WWII, director Maura Delpero crafts a powerful portrait of a family fractured by war and desire. When a Sicilian deserter arrives, his forbidden romance with the eldest daughter unleashes a cascade of moral questions that reverberate through the tightly-knit village. View in Media

item.content:encoded: U.S. Dollar The U.S. Dollar Index climbed to 121.17 in October, reversing a slight decline from September as markets reassess Fed policy and economic growth prospects. Higher rates attract foreign investment in dollar-denominated assets, supporting the currency. But a strong dollar makes American exports more expensive abroad. {{chart:Dollar Value Index}} View all trade indicators

item.title: **How to Have Willpower**
item.content:encoded: How to Have Willpower "then man is born in chains, the innocent and helpless victim of internal passions and external controls that shape and possess him. Personal development, then, is a process of individual liberation, in which self-control and self-direction supplant internal anarchy and external restraint." - Thomas Szasz View in Media

item.title: **La Haine**
item.content:encoded: La Haine Mathieu Kassovitz's 1995 masterpiece follows three young friends—led by Vincent Cassel—navigating the aftermath of a brutal police incident in a Parisian suburb. Shot in stark black-and-white, the film captures 24 hours of systemic tension, urban rage, and the quiet desperation of those living on society's margins. View in Media

item.title: **BLS Employment Gains**
item.description: BLS Employment Gains
item.content:encoded: BLS Employment Gains September saw the addition of 119,000 nonfarm jobs, bringing total employment to 159.626M (+0.1% MoM). The labor market continues steady expansion despite economic uncertainty. Employment continued to trend up in health care, food services and drinking places, and social assistance. Job losses occurred in transportation and warehousing and in federal government. {{chart:Total Nonfarm Employment}} Context: Consistent job growth maintains labor market resilience, though month

item.title: The $2000 Tariff Rebate
item.description: Solicitor General Sauer's argument carries a paradox at its core: its success is measured not by what it collects, but by what it prevents.
item.content:encoded: The $2000 Tariff Rebate Solicitor General Sauer's argument carries a paradox at its core: its success is measured not by what it collects, but by what it prevents. Each avoided payment, each concession extracted abroad, is framed as a regulatory win - proof that pressure works before the tariff ever takes effect. The Theory When foreign competitors believe tariffs could strike at any moment, they move preemptively: - Concede faster in trade negotiations - Accelerate domestic or allied-countr

item.content:encoded: It’s not a donut hole; it’s a different kind of pastry. The Supreme Court's oral arguments on Trump v. item.content:encoded: Federal Court: AAUP v. Rubio ‐ First Amendment for Non-Citizens▪ Date: Sept 30, 2025 ‐ Judge William G. Young▪ Ruling: Court found administration officials engaged in viewpoint discrimination and chilled pro-Palestinian speech; non-citizens entitled to First Amendment protections under existing precedent.▪ Significance: Detailed factual findings, novel use of immigration enforcement for political speech condemned; remedy hearing set (relief limited to subordinate officials).Read full summary on
